key_insight: The paper argues that autoformalization needs faithfulness checking, not just type-correct translation, and proposes Bidirectional Provability Fingerprinting to certify whether a formal statement preserves the intended theorem.
engineering_relevance: This is useful for theorem-proving pipelines because it can screen, score, and even guide generation of Lean statements that are semantically closer to the original natural-language math.
method: Build forward/backward consequence fingerprints for candidate formal statements and compare them to probes derived from the source text.
Use Counterfactual Probe Generation to target specific semantic drift directions and an Equivalence Spectrum to replace binary pass/fail judgments.
Allocate probe budget adaptively and feed faithfulness signals back into decoding as a reward for autoformalization.
evidence_signal: On DriftBench with 2,183 NL/Lean 4 pairs, BPF plus CPG detected 89.6% of drifted formalizations at a 3.0% false-positive rate, outperforming typecheck (41.2%) and LLM-judge (63.3%) baselines; FGD reduced drifted outputs by 47%.
watch_item: The approach depends on well-designed probes and the ambient theory’s consequence structure, so performance may degrade when the target theorem is underspecified or the benchmark does not cover a domain’s drift modes.
paper_title: The Faithfulness Gap: Certifying Semantic Equivalence Between Natural-Language and Formal Mathematical Statements
